prescan.instruction: Before analyzing contract provisions, perceive the document's legal structure:
prescan.step: Identify whether this is a contract, amendment, exhibit, or other legal document.
Identify the document title and contract type from the header.
Note the structural hierarchy: articles, sections, subsections, and their numbering scheme.
Identify the parties from the preamble or header block.
Observe the overall document flow: preamble → recitals → operative provisions → general provisions → signature blocks.

principle.name: visual_hierarchy
principle.guidance: Legal documents use consistent visual hierarchy to signal structure: ALL CAPS or bold text = article headings (ARTICLE I — DEFINITIONS). Numbered sections (1.1, 1.2) = operative provisions. Indented sub-items (a), (b), (c) = enumerated lists within a section. Italic or quoted text = defined terms being introduced. Use this hierarchy to correctly nest and classify provisions.

principle.name: proximity
principle.guidance: Spatial grouping in contracts indicates logical relationships: Text immediately following a section number belongs to that section. Indented items under a section are sub-provisions of that section. Signature blocks grouped together indicate the execution section. Recitals (WHEREAS clauses) cluster between the preamble and operative provisions.

principle.name: similarity
principle.guidance: Consistent formatting indicates provision type: Same numbering depth (1.1, 2.1, 3.1) = same structural level. Same indentation = same hierarchical level. Capitalized terms in quotes = defined terms throughout. Blank lines with underscores = fill-in fields requiring negotiation. Use formatting consistency to classify and group provisions.

principle.name: continuity
principle.guidance: Follow the logical flow of contract provisions: Articles progress from definitions → rights → financial → term → general. Section numbers within an article are sequential. Cross-references (e.g., "as defined in Section 1.2") create logical links between provisions. Defined terms introduced early are used throughout — track their usage.

document_structure_patterns.pattern: Preamble → Recitals → Articles (numbered) → Signature blocks. Most common structure.
Same as standard but with blank fill-in fields (underscores, brackets) indicating negotiable terms.
References an existing agreement by date and parties, then modifies specific provisions.
Supplementary document referenced by the main agreement, containing detailed specifications, lists, or calculations.
Page begins mid-sentence or mid-section, indicating continuation from a previous page.

provision_regions.region: Opening paragraph identifying parties, date, and agreement type. Contains party definitions.
WHEREAS clauses establishing background and purpose. Precede the operative provisions.
Article defining key terms. Usually Article I. Terms are capitalized and quoted.
Core operative provisions granting or licensing rights, including territory, media, language, and exclusivity. The commercial heart of the agreement.
Chain of title, IP ownership warranties, work-for-hire references, and prior encumbrances. May appear in recitals, representations, or a dedicated article.
Compensation, fees, revenue shares, payment terms. The economic provisions.
Delivery, performance, reporting, editorial control, key person, and compliance requirements.
Guild compliance, residual obligations, music licensing, and embedded IP clearances. May be in a dedicated article or scattered across reps/warranties and delivery provisions.
AI training, digital likeness, NFT/metaverse, social media clips, viewer data, and data privacy. Often in a modern addendum or scattered across rights grant and restrictions.
E&O coverage, general liability, additional insured requirements. Often in the legal protections or general provisions article.
Content ratings, local content quotas, accessibility, export controls, data protection law compliance. May be in a dedicated compliance article or in delivery/obligations.
Governing law, dispute resolution, force majeure, entire agreement. General provisions typically near the end.
Execution blocks with party names, signature lines, and date fields.
